stimulus: The Late Ordovician mass extinction, occurring roughly 444 million years ago, eliminated nearly 85% of marine species. Some researchers argue that a nearby supernova’s gamma-ray burst stripped Earth’s ozone layer and triggered the event. Others remain skeptical, partly because no isotopic signature characteristic of a supernova event has been securely dated to the boundary. In 2021, a team led by Reiko Tanaka identified an unusual iron-60 enrichment in deep-sea sediment cores recovered off the Patagonian shelf. Researchers who favor the supernova hypothesis cite this enrichment as supporting evidence. However, Tanaka’s team has not yet been able to determine the depositional age of the iron-60 layer. Consequently, the team cautions that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: iron-60 deposits in deep-sea sediments are generally too rare to support any conclusions about ancient astronomical events.
the discovered iron-60 enrichment cannot be confidently linked to the start of the Late Ordovician extinction.
most other researchers have overlooked the role volcanic activity may have played in the extinction.
the existing dates assigned to the Late Ordovician extinction are themselves likely to be incorrect.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The text notes that some scientists invoke a supernova burst to explain the extinction, and that Tanaka’s team found an iron-60 enrichment that supporters take as evidence. However, the team has not been able to date that layer. Without a secure date, the enrichment cannot be confidently tied to the start of the extinction event.

Choice A is incorrect because the text never claims that iron-60 deposits are too rare to ever support inferences—only that this particular layer cannot yet be dated. Choice C is incorrect because the debate in the text concerns whether the iron-60 layer is contemporaneous with the extinction, not whether volcanism has been overlooked. Choice D is incorrect because the text gives a fixed date (roughly 444 million years ago) for the extinction and never suggests that date is in doubt; the uncertainty lies with the iron-60 layer’s age.
